Press enter or click to view image in full size

10 min read
Just now

--

A developer’s guide to spending less on AI coding tools and knowing exactly what you give up when you do.

In this article, you will learn how to replace a $20/month AI coding subscription with a cheaper setup that still handles most real work. You will see two tiers: cheap-API agents that run on any laptop, and fully local models that cost nothing to run after setup. By the end, you will have a working OpenCode + OpenRouter configuration you can use today.
Prerequisites: You should be comfortable opening a terminal, and you should have VS Code installed. For Tier A (cheap-API), you will need a credit or debit card to add a small balance to OpenRouter. For Tier B (local models), the requirements depend on your hardware, and the article covers what you need.

The Moment the Bill Stopped Making Sense
I have been using Claude Code for about six months, paying for the Claude Pro plan to access it. The tool works, and it works well. But Claude Code costs $20 a month, and heavy users can hit their limits by midweek. There were stretches where I was barely coding, switching between a few simple projects, and the subscription kept running. I started asking a question I probably should have asked sooner: do I actually need to pay this much?
This write-up is not about Claude Code being bad. It is about spending less on purpose, while knowing the trade you are making. Because cheaper is never free. The cost moves. It does not disappear.

First, Be Honest About What You Are Replacing (Claude Code Alternatives Overview)
The goal is not to find a tool that matches Claude Code dollar for dollar in capability. The goal is to find the right tool for the kind of work you are doing right now.
If you are building something that is not complex, or you are going through a stretch where you barely touch your codebase, paying $20 a month for a top-tier agent is a bad trade. The question is what you switch to, and what you give up.
One rule runs through every section below: cheaper always costs you something. The cost moves, but it does not disappear. Sometimes it becomes hardware. Sometimes it becomes slower output. Sometimes it becomes more of your own time steering the tool. That is the honest frame this article is written from.

The Key Insight: The Coding Agent and the AI Model Are Two Different Things
Most developers think of Claude Code as one thing. It is not. There are two parts: the agent and the model. Once you see the difference, the whole cheaper-alternative landscape opens up.
The agent is the body. It reads your repo, plans what to change, edits your files, and runs commands. The model is the brain. It is what reasons about your code and decides what to write.
Claude Code bundles the agent and the model together. You get both in one subscription. But most of the alternatives let you separate them. You pick the agent, then you plug in whichever model you want underneath it. That is the unlock.
The agent is the steering wheel. The model is the engine. You can swap the engine.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once you see it that way, “cheaper” splits into two moves: use a cheaper model via API, or run a free model on your own machine. Those are the two tiers this article covers.

Tier A: Cheap-API Coding Agents (Works on Any Laptop, Costs Pennies Per Day)
This tier is for anyone without a powerful GPU who wants to pay per task instead of per month.
The agents in this tier are free to install and use. You connect them to a model provider and pay only for the tokens you use. That means on a slow week, you spend almost nothing.
Here are the main options, compared:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The pattern: Cline is the most hands-off. Aider is the leanest on tokens. OpenCode sits in the middle and lets you switch models with one command.
One thing worth knowing about Gemini CLI: it was discontinued on June 18, 2026 for free and individual users. Google replaced it with Antigravity CLI. Antigravity does have a free tier and no credit card is required, but the limits are much stricter than Gemini CLI.
The other one I genuinely like, especially as an AWS developer, is Kiro. Kiro is built by AWS, and it takes a spec-driven approach: before any agent writes a line of code. It is not a BYOK “bring your own model” tool like aider or cline, but aside from Claude models, it supports open-weight models like Qwen3 Coder Next and DeepSeek v3.2. If you work with AWS, it is worth testing.
The Models to Plug In
Once you have an agent, you need a model. These three are the ones worth knowing for cheap-API use, all accessible through OpenRouter with one API key:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

All three cost a fraction of what a frontier model charges for the same tokens. The skill is matching the model to the task: a cheap fast one for prototyping, a stronger one when the logic gets hard.

Tier B: Fully Local Models for Coding (Zero Ongoing Cost, Hardware Required)
Running a model locally means zero API cost and total data privacy. Your code never leaves your machine. But it comes with a real upfront cost: hardware.
How to Know Whether a Local Model Will Run on Your Machine
Two things decide whether a model fits on your machine: the parameter count (how large the model is) and your VRAM (how much memory your graphics card has). Here is the rough table:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If the model does not fit in VRAM, it spills into system RAM and slows down hard, around six times slower in practice.
How Quantization Helps You Fit Larger Models on Smaller Hardware
Quantization shrinks a model so it needs less memory. It works by reducing the precision of the model’s weights. The base is 16-bit. Then 8-bit, 4-bit, and lower from there. The smaller you go, the less VRAM you need, but the more you give up on output quality.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

LM Studio, a free GUI tool, makes this easy to navigate. When you browse a model, it shows you the quantization level, how much space it takes, and how much VRAM it needs. You can see at a glance whether it will run on your machine.
How Mixture of Experts Models Help You Run Larger Models Without Full VRAM
One way around the VRAM problem is a Mixture of Experts (MoE) model. An MoE model only activates a small subset of its parameters, called experts, for any given token. The active experts stay on the GPU. The rest sit in system RAM. Because most of the model is idle at any moment, the speed penalty for offloading is much smaller than with a dense model of the same size. LM Studio lets you configure this when you load a model.
What the Research Says About Local Model Quality in 2026
Vicki Boykis wrote a useful piece on this in June 2026, arguing that local models have gotten genuinely good (running local models is good now). A few things I took from it:

• She runs a 64 GB M2 Mac, and even then the KV cache can grow to fill all 64 GB during a long agentic session. That hidden memory cost is easy to underestimate.

• She favors a smaller quantized model (gemma-4–12b-qat) over bigger variants. Newer, smaller, and faster, with little drop in accuracy.

• Recent Gemma models hit roughly 75% of a frontier model’s accuracy. Real progress, but inference is still slower and context windows stay small.

• Her verdict: great for refactoring, writing tests, and bootstrapping projects. Not production-ready yet, but improving fast.
There is a good discussion on this on Hacker News.
The honest caveat: most of those benchmarks come from 128 GB Mac Studios or Linux rigs with 64 GB or more of VRAM. On a typical developer laptop with 16 to 32 GB of RAM, the experience is different.
I have not run a local model myself because of my current hardware, but it is something I plan to try once I upgrade.
How to Set Up a Local Model With Ollama
Ollama is the fastest way to get a local model running:

• Install Ollama from ollama.com.

• Pull a model from the terminal: ollama pull qwen2.5-coder. Swap in whichever model fits your hardware.

• Ollama runs a local server at http://localhost:11434. Point your agent at it. OpenCode, Cline, and Continue all support Ollama as a provider.

• Select the model inside the agent and you are coding fully local, with no API key and no token cost.
You can also use LM Studio if you prefer a GUI. it has it own models browser, and it shows you quantization options, memory requirements, and lets you configure context length before loading.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Kyle Cook {web simplified} talked extensively in this video on local agentic coding workflow; he talked about using LM Studio as a way to load models.
Local is not free. It is prepaid, in hardware.

How I Actually Use This Now (My OpenCode + OpenRouter Setup)
I went with the cheap-API path, not local. The setup I landed on is OpenCode pointed at OpenRouter. It sits between Aider and Cline: more autonomous than Aider, leaner on tokens than Cline. I switch between Qwen, DeepSeek, and Gemini Flash depending on what the task needs. My total spend has stayed well under $10 a month.
Here is the full setup, step by step.
Step 1: Create an OpenRouter account and add credits. Go to openrouter.ai and sign up. OpenRouter is the middleman that gives you one API key to reach almost every model. Click your profile, go to Credits, and add some money. This is pay-as-you-go, not a subscription. I started with $10 and it lasted a while. Then go to Keys, create a new API key, and save it somewhere safe. You only see it once.
Step 2: Open the terminal inside VS Code. Open your project folder in VS Code. Open the built-in terminal with View > Terminal or the shortcut Ctrl+` (backtick key, top left of most keyboards). You run everything from here.
Step 3: Install OpenCode. In the terminal, run:
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bashOr with npm:
npm install -g opencode-aiClose and reopen the terminal when it finishes. Test with opencode --version. If you see a version number, it installed correctly.
Step 4: Connect OpenCode to OpenRouter. Run:
opencode auth loginUse the arrow keys to select OpenRouter and press Enter. Paste your API key when prompted. That is the connection done.
Step 5: Start a session and pick your model. From inside your project folder, type:
opencodeType /models to open a searchable list. Type "qwen" for Qwen3 Coder, "deepseek" for DeepSeek, or "gemini" for Gemini Flash. Select one and start coding. That /models command is the switch I use constantly: a cheap fast model for quick frontend work, then DeepSeek when the logic gets harder. You can watch your OpenRouter balance update in real time on the dashboard.
Install once, paste your key once. Switching models from there is a single command.

Pick Your Setup: A Decision Guide by Budget and Hardware
If you are switching from Claude Code, the session history does not carry over. But your git history and any conventions you saved in a CLAUDE.md file will give the new agent enough context to get started. You keep the code and the commits. You lose the chat history.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The honest verdict: the cheap stack replaced Claude Code for some of my work. Prototyping, frontend work, simple backend tasks. I do not miss the subscription for those. But for complex refactors, production debugging, and multi-file architecture changes, I still open Claude Code. The gap is closing fast. It is not closed yet.
Pick one row from that table and set it up this week.

What You Learned
You now know the difference between an agent and a model, and why that separation is the key to cutting your AI coding costs. You have seen two tiers: cheap-API agents like OpenCode, Cline, and Aider that work on any laptop for pennies a day, and fully local setups through Ollama or LM Studio that cost nothing to run once your hardware can support them
